texts.label: text
texts.orig: ABSTRACT: The permeability of polydimethylsiloxane [PDMS] to acetone, nitrogen, and acetone/nitrogen mixtures has been determined at 28 7 C. In pure gas experiments, the permeability of PDMS to nitrogen was 245 1 10 0 10 cm 3 (STP) r cm/cm 2 r s r cmHg and was independent of pressure. The permeability of PDMS to acetone vapor increased exponentially with increasing acetone pressure. PDMS is much more permeable to acetone than to nitrogen; acetone/nitrogen selectivity increases from 85 to 185 as acetone partial pressure in the feed increases from 0 to 67% of saturation. In mixed gas permeation experiments, the nitrogen permeability coefficient is independent of acetone relative pressure and is equal to the pure gas permeability coefficient. The acetone permeability coefficient has the same value in both mixed gas and pure acetone permeation experiments. Average acetone diffusivity in PDMS, determined as the ratio of permeability to solubility, decreases with increasing acetone concentration due to mild clustering of acetone in the polymer (because acetone is a poor solvent for PDMS) and changes in the polymer-penetrant thermodynamic interactions which influence diffusion coefficients. A Zimm-Lundberg analysis of the acetone sorption isotherm is also consistent with acetone clustering in PDMS. q 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Polym Sci B: Polym Phys 36: 289-301, 1998

texts.orig: Membrane-based separation of vapors from air streams is currently used to recover vinyl chloride monomer, ethylene oxide, various fluorocarbon compounds, ethylene, and propylene vapors from air or nitrogen streams. 3-5 For membrane-based vapor separation applications, polymers are sought that are highly permeable and vapor selective. High permeabilities lead to low membrane area requirements, which reduces capital costs, and high selectivity results in high-purity product streams.

texts.orig: process generates acetone/air mixtures that must be separated to recover and recycle acetone. Our study is aimed at exploring the fundamental transport properties of acetone and nitrogen (a simulant for air) in membrane materials suitable for this separation. PDMS is used commercially for membrane-based vapor separation applications. 3,5 This polymer is the most permeable rubber known and is among the most permeable polymers. 6 Unlike traditional glassy polymers used for the separation of supercritical gases, PDMS is more permeable to many organic vapors than to gases such as oxygen and nitrogen, making it useful for removal of low concentrations of organics from air. 3,4 In this article, the sorption and diffusion of acetone vapor in PDMS and pure and mixed gas permeability properties of PDMS to acetone and N2 are reported.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Solubility typically increases as penetrant condensability, characterized by critical temperature, boiling point, or Lennard-Jones force constant, increases. 8 Therefore, solubility selectivity increases as the difference in condensability between penetrants in a mixture increases. For vapor/gas separations, solubility selectivity is greater than one. Smaller, less condensable, supercritical gas molecules have higher diffusion coefficients than larger, more condensable vapor penetrants. 7 Therefore, diffusivity selectivity is always less than one for vapor/gas separations and becomes smaller as the difference in size of the vapor and gas components increases. There is a tradeoff between solubility selectivity and diffusivity selectivity; whether a membrane is vaporor gas-selective depends on the relative magnitude of the diffusivity and solubility selectivity for the components in the mixture. 9 For a polymer to be more permeable to an organic vapor such as acetone than to a supercritical gas such as nitrogen, the high vapor/gas solubility selectivity must more than offset the vapor/gas diffusivity selectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Filler-free PDMS (Dehesive Silicone 940A, Wacker Silicones Corp., Adrian, MI) composite membranes were used for pure and mixed gas permeation experiments. The composite membranes consisted of a highly microporous support coated with a 28 m m-thick PDMS layer. Crosslinking of the PDMS membrane was carried out at 100 7 C using a proprietary crosslinker/catalyst system supplied by Wacker Silicones Corp.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For acetone sorption in PDMS, a dense, fillerfree PDMS film of 350 m m thickness was used. Crosslinking was achieved using the same components and conditions as those for the composite membranes. The film density was 0.98 g/cm 3 , determined by measuring the weight of a film of known thickness and area. The crosslink density, ( y e )/( V 0 ), was estimated by measuring the amount of liquid cyclohexane sorbed by a dry PDMSfilm of known weight. 12 The value for cyclohexane sorption in uncrosslinked PDMS is 0.432. 13 Using this value for x and the amount of cyclohexane absorbed by the film, the crosslink density of the film was estimated using eq. (5) to be 7.8 1 10 0 5 mol/cm 3 . Because the composite membrane was prepared under identical conditions of temperature, catalyst type, and crosslinker concentration, the crosslink density of the PDMS layer in the composite membrane is presumed to be similar to that of the film.

texts.orig: Gravimetric sorption experiments were performed using a conventional McBain spring balance system, which consists of a glass water-jacketed chamber serviced by a high vacuum line for sample degassing and penetrant removal. 14 Inside the chamber, a PDMS film was suspended from a helical quartz spring supplied by Ruska Laboratories, Inc. (Houston, TX). These springs have a sensitivity of 0.70 mm of extension per mg of load. By monitoring changes in the length of the spring, changes in sample weight are measured. The temperature was controlled to { 0.1 7 C by a constanttemperature water bath.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The polymer sample to be studied was placed in the sample chamber of the sorption system and exposed to vacuum for at least 24 h to remove air gases. Acetone vapor was then introduced to the sample chamber at a fixed pressure, and the subsequent increase in sample weight was recorded. When the sample weight had been constant for several hours, more acetone was introduced into the sorption chamber to begin the next experiment. From these data, acetone uptake was determined as a function of acetone pressure in the vapor phase surrounding the sample. 15

texts.orig: These vapor phase sorption data were complemented by a determination of the amount of liquid acetone absorbed by the PDMS film at 28 7 C. A film sample of known weight was soaked in acetone overnight to ensure that equilibrium had been attained. The film was removed from the acetone solution, acetone on the surface of the film was removed by blotting the film briefly with paper tissue, and the acetone-laden film was weighed. This procedure was repeated several times to obtain an accurate estimate of the acetone sorption level at unit activity (i.e., p / po 1).

texts.orig: ments. Experiments were carried out at 28 7 Cwith acetone/nitrogen mixtures at upstream pressures ranging from 10 to 12 atmospheres and with the downstream pressure fixed at atmospheric pressure. A 47-mm in-line stainless filter holder from Millipore Corporation (Bedford, MA) was used as the permeation cell for all permeability measurements. Acetone/nitrogen mixtures were prepared in a vapor/gas generator, which consisted of a pressure vessel, in an isothermal bath, containing liquid acetone and serviced by high-pressure lines to supply nitrogen and deliver the nitrogen/acetone mixture. Nitrogen was bubbled through acetone in the generator. This acetone-laced nitrogen stream was then blended with pure nitrogen to form a mixture of the desired composition. The mixture composition was held constant by using a mass flow controller (Brooks Mass Flow Controller, Model 5850E) purchased from Scott Specialty Gases (Durham, NC) to control the flowrate of pure nitrogen to the vapor/gas generator.

Nitrogen was bubbled through acetone in the generator. This acetone-laced nitrogen stream was then blended with pure nitrogen to form a mixture of the desired composition. The mixture composition was held constant by using a mass flow controller (Brooks Mass Flow Controller, Model 5850E) purchased from Scott Specialty Gases (Durham, NC) to control the flowrate of pure nitrogen to the vapor/gas generator.

texts.text: Sorption of Acetone Vapors in PDMS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 1 presents the acetone sorption isotherm for a PDMS film as a function of acetone relative pressure. The isotherm is convex to the relative pressure axis, and the slope of the isotherm increases as relative pressure increases. This isotherm shape is commonly observed for sorption of organic vapors in rubbery polymers. 7,16 In crosslinked rubbery polymers, such isotherms are often described using the model presented in eq. (5). The data reported in Figure 1 could not be satisfactorily fit to eq. (5) with a constant value of the interaction parameter, x . For many polymer/ penetrant systems, x is known to depend on concentration and often decreases with increasing penetrant concentration. 17 Decreasing x values with increasing penetrant concentration indicate that the environment into which the penetrant molecules are dissolving is becoming more like that of pure liquid penetrant. The concentration of acetone dissolved in the polymer increases as x decreases, consistent with the notion that the polymer/penetrant mixture is an environment more compatible with acetone than the polymer alone.

texts.orig: Consistent with our findings, Baker et al. report acetone permeability coefficients of PDMS to be 9900 Barrers at a relative pressure of 0.02 and 19,000 Barrers at a relative pressure of 0.27. 3 These measurements were made using PDMS manufactured by Dow Corning, and the temperature was 40 7 C. From Figure 4, we estimate acetone permeability coefficients of 21,300 and 26,800 Barrers at relative pressures of 0.02 and 0.27, respectively. The lower temperature of our measurements contributes to the higher permeability coefficients in our experiments. Permeability coefficients of organic penetrants in PDMS decrease with increasing temperature (i.e., the activation energy of permeation is negative). For example, the activation energies of permeation for n -butane and n -pentane in PDMS are 0 1,070 and 0 1,960 cal/mol, respectively. 19

The uncertainty in L is estimated, by a propagation of error analysis, 21 to be { 0.4 1 10 0 6 cm 2 / s for D eff data and 0.3 1 10 0 6 cm 2 / s for D V data. The penetrant mobility estimated from both the effective and concentrationaveraged diffusion coefficients generally decreases with increasing penetrant concentration. Typically, penetrant mobility increases with increasing penetrant concentration. A decrease in penetrant mobility with increasing penetrant concentration is indicative of increased frictional resistance to penetrant diffusion through the polymer. For the PDMS-acetone system, this may imply weak clustering of acetone molecules. Because acetone clusters would be larger than single acetone molecules, the mobility of these clusters would be lower than that of pure acetone, thereby decreasing the mobility coefficient. These data and the clustering analysis below suggest that acetone undergoes mild clustering in the PDMS matrix. The very poor solvent quality of acetone for PDMS leads naturally to nonrandom mixing of acetone molecules and PDMS segments, 25 which could result in mobility factor values that decrease with increasing concentration.

texts.orig: for a two-component system. In this equation G 11 is the cluster integral, and V V 1 is the penetrant partial molar volume. The excess penetrant concentration factor, f G 11 / V V 1, is the average number of penetrant molecules in excess of the average penetrant concentration in the neighborhood of a given penetrant molecule. 27 The quantity (1 / f ( G 11 )/( V V 1)) is an estimate of the cluster size (i.e., the average number of penetrant molecules in a cluster). For ideal solutions, G 11 / V V 1 has a value of 0 1, indicating that a penetrant molecule excludes its own volume, V V 1 , to other polymer and penetrant molecules but does not otherwise influence the distribution of other molecules in the polymer/penetrant system. In this case, penetrant clustering does not occur (i.e., the arrangement of molecules is completely random). For a rubbery polymer-penetrant mixture, ( G 11 )/( V V 1 ) 0 when x 0, and signifies clustering sufficient to overcome the excluding effect of an isolated penetrant molecule. 28 Thus, even in athermal polymer solutions, there is a natural segregation of penetrant molecules and polymer segments. The connectivity of polymer segments leads to a nonrandom distribution of penetrant and polymer segments (i.e., clustering). When G 11 / V V 1 is greater than 0, the presence of a penetrant molecule increases the probability that other penetrant molecules will be nearby. That is, penetrant molecules tend to cluster, or the penetrant concentration tends to be higher in the neighborhood of a penetrant than could be expected based on a random dispersion of the penetrant molecules throughout the polymer sample. When the Zimm-Lundberg excess penetrant concentration factor is greater than zero, G 11 / V V 1 must be positive, which indicates penetrant clustering in the polymer.

texts.orig: Figure 8 presents the excess penetrant concentration factor, f ( G 11 )/( V V 1 ), as a function of acetone relative pressure. Values of the factor were calculated using two methods: (1) applying eq. (27) directly by numerical differentiation of the sorption data, and (2) using eq. (28) and the concentration dependence of x given by eq. (11). There is excellent agreement between the two methods. The values of f ( G 11 )/( V V 1 ) are positive and increase with increasing acetone relative pressure, suggesting that clustering of acetone molecules is occurring and becomes more extensive at higher acetone relative pressure. The increase in clustering as penetrant activity increases is consistent with the observations of Zimm and Lundberg for benzene-rubber and toluene-polystyrene systems. 26 Thus, both equilibrium solubility data and diffusion data, inferred from permeability and solubility data, suggest mild acetone clustering in PDMS. These results also support the trends in the mobility of acetone in PDMS (presented in Fig. 7). As acetone concentration increases, the excess penetrant concentration factor predicts larger clusters, which is consistent with the decrease in penetrant mobility at high acetone concentrations in the polymer.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: CONCLUSIONS
texts.text: CONCLUSIONS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sorption isotherms of pure acetone vapor in PDMS were convex to the acetone relative pressure axis, and could be described using a modified version of the Flory-Huggins model with a concentration-dependent interaction parameter. Permeability of PDMS to pure acetone vapor increased exponentially with acetone relative pressure, due to increasing acetone solubility as relative pressure increased. Based on the concentration dependence of acetone mobility in PDMS, acetone exhibits mild clustering in the polymer matrix at high acetone concentrations in the polymer. PDMS was highly selective for acetone in acetone/N2 mixtures, and selectivity increased with increasing acetone relative pressure. The permeability coefficients of both acetone and N2 in the mixture were sensibly equal to the pure gas permeability coefficients, indicating that the transport of each component in the mixture is independent of the presence of the other component.
texts.text: Sorption isotherms of pure acetone vapor in PDMS were convex to the acetone relative pressure axis, and could be described using a modified version of the Flory-Huggins model with a concentration-dependent interaction parameter. Permeability of PDMS to pure acetone vapor increased exponentially with acetone relative pressure, due to increasing acetone solubility as relative pressure increased. Based on the concentration dependence of acetone mobility in PDMS, acetone exhibits mild clustering in the polymer matrix at high acetone concentrations in the polymer. PDMS was highly selective for acetone in acetone/N2 mixtures, and selectivity increased with increasing acetone relative pressure. The permeability coefficients of both acetone and N2 in the mixture were sensibly equal to the pure gas permeability coefficients, indicating that the transport of each component in the mixture is independent of the presence of the other component.

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: * A propagation of errors analysis 21 was employed to estimate the uncertainty in the mixed gas permeabilities and selectivities presented in this and subsequent figures. The largest source of error is the determination of acetone concentration in the feed and permeate streams. Because PDMS is highly selective and the upstream to downstream pressure ratio is only 10-12, acetone partial pressure in the feed and permeate streams are nearly equal. Therefore, rather small uncertainties in feed and permeate concentrations can lead to large uncertainties in acetone permeability coefficients in the mixture.
texts.text: * A propagation of errors analysis 21 was employed to estimate the uncertainty in the mixed gas permeabilities and selectivities presented in this and subsequent figures. The largest source of error is the determination of acetone concentration in the feed and permeate streams. Because PDMS is highly selective and the upstream to downstream pressure ratio is only 10-12, acetone partial pressure in the feed and permeate streams are nearly equal. Therefore, rather small uncertainties in feed and permeate concentrations can lead to large uncertainties in acetone permeability coefficients in the mixture.
